title: Congruence & transformations
description: Given a pair of figures in the coordinate plane, determine whether they are congruent based on whether it is possible to map one to the other using rigid transformations.

assessment_items.question_content:
Yuki was curious if $\triangle ABC$ was congruent to $\triangle FED$, so she tried to map one triangle onto the other using transformations:



[[☃ image 1]]

Yuki concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the triangles are **not** congruent."

**What error did Yuki make in her conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's see if a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) can map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. If it is possible, then the triangles are congruent.

There are many possible sequences of transformations that may map one figure onto another.

Yuki translated point $B$ onto point $E$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Is there another rigid transformation we could now use to complete the mapping?

Let's try rotating by $180^\circ$ about point $E$.



[[☃ image 1]]

The pre-image $\triangle ABC$ maps exactly onto $\triangle FED$.

Yuki concluded, "It's not possible to map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the triangles are **not** congruent," but she made an error.

**One more transformation — a rotation — would map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. So the triangles are congruent.**

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. A pre-image Triangle A B C has point A at two, two, point B at five, four, and Point C at eleven, two. Another triangle D E F has point D at three, twelve, point E at nine, ten, and point F at twelve twelve. Triangle A B C is translated four units to the right and six units up to form the image Triangle A prime at six, eight, B prime at nine, ten, and C prime at fifteen, eight.

choices.content: One more transformation — a rotation — would map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. So the triangles **are** congruent.
One more transformation — a reflection — would map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. So the triangles **are** congruent.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Adam was curious if quadrilaterals $ABCD$ and $GFEH$ were congruent, so he tried to map one figure onto the other using transformations:



[[☃ image 1]]

Adam concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $ABCD$ onto $GFEH$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the quadrilaterals are **not** congruent."

**What error did Adam make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's see if a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) can map $ABCD$ onto $GFEH$. If it is possible, then the quadrilaterals are congruent.

There are many possible sequences of transformations that may map one figure onto another.

Adam translated point $A$ onto point $G$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Is there another rigid transformation we could now use to complete the mapping?

Let's try reflecting over side $\overline{GF}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

The pre-image $ABCD$ maps exactly onto $GFEH$.

Adam concluded, "It's not possible to map $ABCD$ onto $GFEH$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the quadrilaterals are **not** congruent," but he made an error.

**One more transformation — a reflection — would map $ABCD$ onto $GFEH$. So the quadrilaterals are congruent.**

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. A pre-image quadrilateral A B C D has point A at ten, eight, point B at fourteen, eight, and Point C at fourteen, six, and Point D at eleven, six. Another quadrilateral E F G H has point E at six, fourteen, point F at six, twelve, point G at two twelve, and point H at three, fourteen. Quadrilateral A B C D is translated eight units to the left and four units up to form the image Quadrilateral A prime B prime C prime D prime has point A prime at two, twelve, point B prime at six, twelve, and Point C prime at six, ten, and Point D prime at three, ten.

choices.content: One more transformation — a rotation — would map $ABCD$ onto $GFEH$. So the quadrilaterals **are** congruent.
One more transformation — a reflection — would map $ABCD$ onto $GFEH$. So the quadrilaterals **are** congruent.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

[[☃ image 1]]

Is there another rigid transformation we could now use to complete the mapping?

Let's try reflecting over a vertical line that passes through the midpoint of $\overline{DE}$.

Another triangle D E F has point D at three, three, point E at eleven, three, and point F at five, eleven. Triangle A B C is rotated to form the image Triangle A prime at nine, eleven, B prime at three, three, and C prime at eleven, three.

choices.content: One more transformation — a rotation — would map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. So the triangles **are** congruent.
One more transformation — a reflection — would map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. So the triangles **are** congruent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jerome was curious if $\triangle ABC$ was congruent to $\triangle FED$, so he tried to map one triangle onto the other using transformations:



[[☃ image 1]]

Jerome concluded:  

"It's not possible to map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the triangles are **not** congruent."

**What error did Jerome make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's see if a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) can map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. If it is possible, then the triangles are congruent.

There are many possible sequences of transformations that may map one figure onto another.

Jerome translated point $B$ onto point $E$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Are there other rigid transformations we could now use to complete the mapping?

We successfully mapped $\overline{AB}$ to $\overline{FE}$, so let's try reflecting over $\overleftrightarrow{FE}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

This doesn't map one figure onto the other.

To keep $\overline{AB}$ mapped onto $\overline{FE}$, let's try rotating $180 \degree$ about the midpoint of $\overline{FE}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

This doesn't map one figure onto the other.

Notice that $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle FED$ have different side lengths and angle measures, so they can't be congruent.

Jerome concluded, "It's not possible to map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the triangles are **not** congruent."

**There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.**

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. A pre-image Triangle A B C has point A at nine, six, point B at nine, two, and Point C at fourteen, five. Another triangle D E F has point D at two, twelve, point E at seven, ten, and point F at seven, fourteen. Triangle A B C is translated two units to the left and eight units up to form the image Triangle A prime at seven, fourteen, B prime at seven, ten, and C prime at twelve, thirteen.

choices.content: One more transformation — a rotation — would map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. So the triangles **are** congruent.
One more transformation — a reflection — would map $\triangle ABC$ onto $\triangle FED$. So the triangles **are** congruent.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tammy was curious if quadrilaterals $ABCD$ and $FGHE$ were congruent. She was able to map one figure onto the other using a reflection and a translation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Tammy concluded:  

"I was able to map quadrilateral $ABCD$ onto $FGHE$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the figures **are** congruent."

**What error did Tammy make in her conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) can map $ABCD$ onto $FGHE$, then the figures are congruent.

Tammy successfully mapped $ABCD$ onto $FGHE$. Most importantly, she did this using a reflection and a translation, which are both rigid transformations. So the figures are congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

Tammy concluded, "I was able to map quadrilateral $ABCD$ onto $FGHE$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the figures **are** congruent."

**There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.**

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. A pre-image quadrilateral A B C D has point A at two, five, point B at six, five, and Point C at seven, two, and Point D at three, two. Another quadrilateral E F G H has point E at ten, fourteen, point F at nine, eleven, point G at thirteen, eleven, and point H at fourteen, fourteen. Quadrilateral A B C D is reflected across side A B then translated seven units to the right and six units up to form the image Quadrilateral A prime B prime C prime D prime has point A prime at nine, eleven, point B prime at thirteen, eleven, Point C prime at fourteen, fourteen, and Point D prime at ten, fourteen.

choices.content: Tammy didn't use only rigid transformations, so the figures are **not** congruent.
It's possible to map quadrilateral $ABCD$ onto $FGHE$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, but the figures are **not** congruent.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Circles $A$ and $B$ coincide.



[[☃ image 1]]

Alex concluded:  

"Circles $A$ and $B$ have no sides or angles with the same measurement, so the circles are **not** congruent."

**What error did Alex make in their conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) can map circle $A$ onto circle $B$, then the figures are congruent.

We don't need any transformations! These circles already coincide (they are on top of each other).



[[☃ image 1]]

Alex concluded, "Circles $A$ and $B$ have no sides or angles with the same measurement, so the circles are **not** congruent," but they made an error.

**The circles are mapped onto each other, so they are congruent.**

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one. A circle with a center at eight, eight  and a radius of seven. Another circle is mapped on top of the first circle with a center at eight, eight and a radius of seven.

choices.content: The circles are mapped onto each other, so they **are** congruent.
The circles are **not** congruent, because no transformations were used.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Pete was able to map quadrilateral $ABCD$ onto $EFGH$ using a translation and a dilation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Pete concluded:  

"I was able to map quadrilateral $ABCD$ onto $EFGH$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the figures **are** congruent."

**What error did Pete make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) can map $ABCD$ onto $EFGH$, then the figures are congruent.

Pete successfully mapped $ABCD$ onto $EFGH$. But he used a dilation, which isn't a rigid transformation.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can see that $ABCD$ and $EFGH$ have different side lengths, so they can't be congruent.

Pete concluded, "I was able to map quadrilateral $ABCD$ onto $EFGH$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the figures **are** congruent," but he made an error.

**Pete didn't use only rigid transformations, so the figures are not congruent. **

A pre-image quadrilateral A B C D has point A at fourteen, ten, point B at ten, ten, and Point C at eight, fourteen, and Point D at twelve, fourteen. Another quadrilateral E F G H has point E at five, four, point F at three, four, point G at two, six, and point H at four, six. Quadrilateral A B C D is translated six units to the left and eight units down then dilated so that A prime is at five, four, B prime is at three, four, C prime is at two, six, and D prime is at four, six.

choices.content: Pete didn't use only rigid transformations, so the figures are **not** congruent.
It's possible to map quadrilateral $ABCD$ onto $EFGH$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, but the figures are **not** congruent.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:

We can see that circles $M$ and $N$ have different diameters, so they can't be congruent.

Layla concluded, "I was able to map circle $M$ onto circle $N$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the figures **are** congruent," but he made an error.

The second pre image for the circle M has a radius of two units and is a translation of four units left and three units down so that it has the same center as circle N. The image of circle M has a radius of seven units after a dilation.

It's possible to map circle $M$ onto circle $N$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, but the figures are **not** congruent.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

assessment_items.question_content:
Stefan was able to map $\triangle ABD$ onto $\triangle ABC$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Stefan concluded:  

"I was able to map $\triangle ABD$ onto $\triangle ABC$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the figures **are** congruent."

**What error did Stefan make in his conclusion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If a sequence of [[☃ definition 1]] (translations, reflections, and rotations) can map $\triangle ABD$ onto $\triangle ABC$, then the figures are congruent.

Stefan successfully mapped $\triangle ABD$ onto $\triangle ABC$, but he didn't use rigid transformations. He translated some points more than others. Notice how point $D$ traveled farther than points closer to $A$ did, and points $A$ and $B$ didn't move at all.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can see that $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle ABC$ have different side lengths and angle measures, so they can't be congruent.

Stefan concluded, "I was able to map $\triangle ABD$ onto $\triangle ABC$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, so the figures **are** congruent," but he made an error.

**Stefan didn't use only rigid transformations, so the figures are not congruent. **

choices.content: Stefan didn't use only rigid transformations, so the figures are **not** congruent.
It's possible to map $\triangle ABD$ onto $\triangle ABC$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, but the figures are **not** congruent.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.

It's possible to map $\overline{ST}$ onto $\overline{QR}$ using a sequence of rigid transformations, but the figures are **not** congruent.
There is no error. This is a correct conclusion.
